Ayahs

قُلْ أُوحِىَ إِلَىَّ أَنَّهُ ٱسْتَمَعَ نَفَرٌ مِّنَ ٱلْجِنِّ فَقَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّا سَمِعْنَا قُرْءَانًا عَجَبًا (١)
Say, ‘It has been revealed to me that a team of the jinn listened [to the Quran] and they said, “Indeed we heard a wonderful Quran, (1)
يَهْدِىٓ إِلَى ٱلرُّشْدِ فَـَٔامَنَّا بِهِۦ ۖ وَلَن نُّشْرِكَ بِرَبِّنَآ أَحَدًا (٢)
which guides to rectitude. Hence, we have believed in it and we will never ascribe any partner to our Lord. (2)
وَأَنَّهُۥ تَعَـٰلَىٰ جَدُّ رَبِّنَا مَا ٱتَّخَذَ صَـٰحِبَةً وَلَا وَلَدًا (٣)
Exalted be the majesty of our Lord; He has taken neither any spouse nor son. (3)
وَأَنَّهُۥ كَانَ يَقُولُ سَفِيهُنَا عَلَى ٱللَّهِ شَطَطًا (٤)
The foolish ones among us used to speak atrocious lies concerning Allah. (4)
وَأَنَّا ظَنَنَّآ أَن لَّن تَقُولَ ٱلْإِنسُ وَٱلْجِنُّ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ كَذِبًا (٥)
We thought that humans and jinn would never utter any falsehood concerning Allah. (5)
وَأَنَّهُۥ كَانَ رِجَالٌ مِّنَ ٱلْإِنسِ يَعُوذُونَ بِرِجَالٍ مِّنَ ٱلْجِنِّ فَزَادُوهُمْ رَهَقًا (٦)
Indeed some persons from the humans would seek the protection of some persons from the jinn, thus only adding to their rebellion. (6)

Content of Surah "Al-Jinn"

This Surah, as its name suggests, primarily speaks about an unseen creation called "Jinn." It discusses their faith in the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله), their humility before the Majestic Quran, their faith and belief in the Resurrection, the existence of groups of believers and disbelievers among them, and similar matters. This section of the Surah encompasses nineteen out of the twenty-eight verses and corrects many superstitious beliefs regarding "Jinn," marking them as falsehoods.

In another part of this Surah, there is a reference to the issue of Monotheism (Tawhid) and the Resurrection (Ma'ad).

In the final part of this Surah, the discussion turns to the matter of the Knowledge of the Unseen (Ghayb), which no one is aware of except what Allah has willed.

Virtue of Reciting This Surah

In a hadith from the Noble Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله), it is stated:

من قرء سورة الجن اعطى بعدد كل جنى و شيطان صدق بمحمد (ص) و كذب به عتق رقبة:

"Whoever recites Surah 'Al-Jinn' will be given the reward of freeing a slave for every Jinn and Shaytan who either believed in Muhammad or disbelieved in him." [^1] And in another hadith from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام), it is mentioned:

من اكثر قراءة قل اوحى‌

[^1]: "Majma' al-Bayan," Vol. 10, p. 365.

لم يصبه فى الحياة الدنيا شى‌ء من اعين الجن و لا نفثهم و لا سحرهم، و لا كيدهم، و كان مع محمد (ص) فيقول يا رب لا اريد منه بدلا و لا ابغى عنه حولا:

"Whoever recites Surah Al-Jinn frequently will never be afflicted in the life of this world by the evil eye of the Jinn, nor their breath (sorcery), nor their magic, nor their plots. He will be with Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله) and will say: 'O Lord! I desire no substitute for him, and I never seek to turn away from him toward another.'" [^1] Of course, this recitation is a prelude to understanding the content of the Surah and subsequently putting it into practice.

[^1]: "Tafsir al-Burhan," Vol. 4, p. 390.

بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ‌

قُلْ أُوحِيَ إِلَيَّ أَنَّهُ اسْتَمَعَ نَفَرٌ مِنَ الْجِنِّ فَقالُوا إِنَّا سَمِعْنا قُرْآناً عَجَباً (1) يَهْدِي إِلَى الرُّشْدِ فَآمَنَّا بِهِ وَ لَنْ نُشْرِكَ بِرَبِّنا أَحَداً (2) وَ أَنَّهُ تَعالى‌ جَدُّ رَبِّنا مَا اتَّخَذَ صاحِبَةً وَ لا وَلَداً (3) وَ أَنَّهُ كانَ يَقُولُ سَفِيهُنا عَلَى اللَّهِ شَطَطاً (4)

وَ أَنَّا ظَنَنَّا أَنْ لَنْ تَقُولَ الْإِنْسُ وَ الْجِنُّ عَلَى اللَّهِ كَذِباً (5) وَ أَنَّهُ كانَ رِجالٌ مِنَ الْإِنْسِ يَعُوذُونَ بِرِجالٍ مِنَ الْجِنِّ فَزادُوهُمْ رَهَقاً (6)

Occasion of Revelation

In the commentary of verses 29 to 32 of Surah Al-Ahqaf, certain occasions of revelation were mentioned that are completely consistent with the topics of the Surah under discussion (Surah Al-Jinn), indicating that both refer to the same event. The occasions of revelation are summarized as follows:

  1. The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) traveled from Mecca toward the "Ukaz" market in "Ta'if" to invite people to Islam at that great gathering center. However, no one responded positively to his call. On his return, he reached a place called the "Valley of the Jinn." He stayed there for the night and recited verses of the Quran. A group of Jinn heard it, believed, and returned to their people to propagate the message. [^1]

[^1]: Tafsir of "Ali ibn Ibrahim" as quoted in "Nur al-Thaqalayn," Vol. 5, p. 19 (abridged).

  1. Ibn Abbas says: "The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) was busy with the morning prayer and was reciting the Quran. A group of Jinn were investigating the reason why news from the heavens had been cut off from them. They heard the sound of Muhammad's (صلى الله عليه وآله) Quranic recitation and said: 'This is the reason the news of the heavens has been cut off from us.' They returned to their people and invited them to Islam." [^1]

  2. After the death of Abu Talib, things became difficult for the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله). He set out for Ta'if to find supporters. The nobles of Ta'if severely rejected him and threw so many stones at the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) from behind that blood flowed from his blessed feet. Tired and distressed, he came to the side of a garden, and eventually, the slave of the garden owners, whose name was "Addas," believed in him. As the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) was returning to Mecca, he reached a place near a palm tree at night, began to pray, and it was here that a group of Jinn from the people of "Nasibin" or "Yemen" were passing by. They heard the sound of the Prophet's recitation during the morning prayer and believed. [^2]

A group of commentators have cited similar occasions of revelation at the beginning of Surah Al-Jinn, but another occasion is mentioned here that differs from them. It is that Abdullah ibn Mas'ud was asked: "Was any of you companions of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) present with him during the events of the Night of the Jinn?" He said: "None of us was there. One night in Mecca, we could not find the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), and no matter how much we searched, we saw no trace of him. We feared that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) might have been killed. We went searching for him in the valleys of Mecca,

[^1]: "Sahih Bukhari," "Muslim," and "Musnad Ahmad" as quoted in "Fi Zilal," Vol. 7, p. 429 (abridged). [^2]: "Majma' al-Bayan," Vol. 9, p. 92 and "Sirah Ibn Hisham," Vol. 2, pp. 62-63 (abridged).

when suddenly we saw him coming from the direction of Mount Hira. We said: 'Where were you, O Messenger of Allah? We were deeply worried, and last night was the worst night of our lives.' He said: 'A caller from the Jinn came to me, and I went to recite the Quran for them.'" [^1]

Commentary

We Have Heard a Wondrous Quran!

Now, considering what has been said, we return to the commentary of the verses:

In the first verse, He says: "Say, it has been revealed to me that a group of Jinn listened to my words and said: 'Indeed, we have heard a wondrous Quran'" (قُلْ أُوحِيَ إِلَيَّ أَنَّهُ اسْتَمَعَ نَفَرٌ مِنَ الْجِنِّ فَقالُوا إِنَّا سَمِعْنا قُرْآناً عَجَباً). [^2] The expression "أُوحِيَ إِلَيَ" (it has been revealed to me) shows that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) did not personally see the individuals of the Jinn in this incident; rather, he became aware of their listening to the Majestic Quran through revelation.

Furthermore, this verse clearly shows that the tribe of "Jinn" possesses intellect, consciousness, understanding, and perception, as well as religious obligation and responsibility. They are familiar with language and notice the difference between miraculous speech and others. They also consider themselves duty-bound to propagate the truth and are among those addressed by the Quranic calls.

This is part of the characteristics of this unseen living being that can be derived from this verse alone. They have other characteristics as well, which, Allah willing, we will explain at the end of this discussion.

[^1]: "Majma' al-Bayan," Vol. 10, p. 368. [^2]: "Nafar," according to a group of linguists and commentators, refers to 3 to 9 individuals.

They were right to consider the Quran a "wondrous" speech, for its tone and melody are wondrous, as are its influence and attraction. Its content and impact are wondrous, and so is its bringer, who was unschooled and rose from among the unlettered.

It is a speech that is wondrous in both its outward and inward aspects, differing from any other speech, and in this way, they confessed to the miraculous nature of the Quran.

Following this sentence, they spoke other words to their people, which the Quran expresses in the subsequent verses in twelve sentences, each starting with "Inna" (Indeed), which is a sign of emphasis. [^1] First, He says: "They said: 'This Quran guides everyone to the right path, and therefore we have believed in it. And we will never associate anyone with our Lord'" (يَهْدِي إِلَى الرُّشْدِ فَآمَنَّا بِهِ وَ لَنْ نُشْرِكَ بِرَبِّنا أَحَداً).

The expression "Rushd" (Right Guidance) is a very broad and comprehensive term that encompasses every kind of excellence. It is a straight path, without twists or turns, clear and obvious, leading those who traverse it to the destination of happiness and perfection.

[^1]: It is well-known among grammarians that "Inna" in the context of reported speech should be read with a kasra (i), and in the above verses, it is initially with a kasra. However, in the subsequent verses that are conjoined to it, it appears with a fatha (a). Therefore, many commentators have found it necessary to assume omitted words or other justifications. But what prevents us from saying that this grammatical rule has an exception, namely that in cases where it is conjoined to reported speech, it is permissible to read it with a fatha, and the proof is the verses of this Surah.

After expressing faith and negating any form of polytheism, they continued their words regarding the attributes of God as follows: "And exalted is the majestic station of our Lord (from similarity to creatures and from any defect or deficiency), and He has never taken for Himself a mate or a child" (وَ أَنَّهُ تَعالى‌ جَدُّ رَبِّنا مَا اتَّخَذَ صاحِبَةً وَ لا وَلَداً).

"Jadd" in the language has many meanings, including: "greatness," "intensity," "seriousness," "benefit and portion," "becoming new," and the like. However, its original root, as Raghib mentions in "Al-Mufradat," means "cutting." Since every great being is "cut off" (distinct) from other beings, this word has come to mean greatness.

Similarly, regarding its other meanings, the same correlation can be considered.

And if a grandfather is called "Jadd," it is also because of the greatness of his status or age.

A group of commentators have mentioned more limited meanings for "Jadd" here; some interpreted it as "attributes," some as "power," some as "sovereignty and government," some as "blessing," and some as "name," all of which are gathered under the meaning of greatness.

However, since this expression nonetheless brings to mind the common meaning of "Jadd," which is "grandfather," it is mentioned in some narrations that the tribe of Jinn chose such an inappropriate expression out of ignorance—meaning you should never use such an expression regarding Allah. [^1] This hadith might refer to cases where such an association exists.

Otherwise, the Quran quotes the words of the Jinn in these verses with an approving tone and validates them. Moreover, in some sermons of "Nahj al-Balagha," this expression has also been used.

As we read in Sermon 191:

الحمد للَّه الفاشى فى الخلق حمده و الغالب جنده، و المتعالى جده:

"Praise be to Allah, whose praise has spread among the creation, whose army is victorious, and whose majesty (Jadd) is exalted."

In some narrations, it is also mentioned that Anas ibn Malik said:

كان الرجل اذا قرء سورة البقرة جد فى اعيننا: "When a man would learn and recite Surah Al-Baqarah, he would become great (Jadda) in our eyes." [^2] In any case, the use of this word in the sense of majesty and greatness is something that is consistent both with linguistic texts and its instances of usage.

It is noteworthy that the speakers among the Jinn here specifically emphasize that Allah has no mate or child, and this expression likely refers to the negation of a superstition that existed among the Arabs. They used to say: "The angels are the daughters of Allah from a mate among the Jinn whom Allah chose for Himself!" This same meaning is mentioned as a possibility in the commentary of verse 158 of Surah As-Saffat:

[^1]: "Majma' al-Bayan," Vol. 10, p. 368 and "Nur al-Thaqalayn," Vol. 5, p. 435; this expression is referred to in the Tafsir of "Ali ibn Ibrahim." [^2]: "Tafsir al-Qurtubi," Vol. 10, p. 6801.

وَ جَعَلُوا بَيْنَهُ وَ بَيْنَ الْجِنَّةِ نَسَباً: "And they claimed a kinship between Him and the Jinn."

Then they added: "We now confess that the foolish among us used to say outrageous things about Allah, far from the truth" (وَ أَنَّهُ كانَ يَقُولُ سَفِيهُنا عَلَى اللَّهِ شَطَطاً).

The term "foolish" (Safih) here might have a generic and collective meaning, i.e., our fools used to attribute a mate and children to Allah, and had chosen peers and partners for Him, deviating from the path of truth and speaking extravagantly.

Many commentators have also suggested the possibility that "Safih" here has an individual concept and refers to "Iblis," who, after disobeying Allah's command, attributed improper things to His Holy Presence. He even officially protested the Lord's command to prostrate to Adam, considering it far from wisdom, and imagined himself superior to Adam.

Since "Iblis" was from the Jinn, the believing Jinn express their loathing of him in this way and call his words extravagant and "Shatat," for although he was apparently a scholar and a worshiper, a scholar without action and a self-centered, deviant, and arrogant worshiper is a clear instance of a "fool."

"Shatat" (on the weight of wasat) means exceeding the limit of moderation and falling far away. Therefore, words that are far from the truth are called "Shatat," and for the same reason, the banks of large rivers that are far from the water and have high walls are called "Shatt."

Then they added: "We thought that humans and Jinn would never utter a lie against Allah" (وَ أَنَّا ظَنَنَّا أَنْ لَنْ تَقُولَ الْإِنْسُ وَ الْجِنُّ عَلَى اللَّهِ كَذِباً).

This statement might refer to the blind imitation that this group previously had of others, attributing partners, peers, a mate, and children to Allah. They say:

If we accepted these matters from others without proof, it was due to our gullibility; we never imagined that humans and Jinn would dare to attribute such great lies to Allah. But now that we have entered the stage of investigation and realized the truth and believed in it, we now realize the wrongness of this improper imitation, and in this way, we confess to our mistake and the deviation of the polytheistic Jinn.

Then they added: Another deviation of Jinn and humans was that "men from among the humans used to seek refuge with men from among the Jinn, and they caused them to increase in error, sin, and rebellion" (وَ أَنَّهُ كانَ رِجالٌ مِنَ الْإِنْسِ يَعُوذُونَ بِرِجالٍ مِنَ الْجِنِّ فَزادُوهُمْ رَهَقاً).

"Rahaq" (on the weight of shafaq) originally means covering something with force and dominance. Since "error," "sin," "rebellion," and "fear" dominate the heart and soul of man and cover it, it has been interpreted with these meanings.

A group of commentators have considered this sentence a reference to another superstition that existed in the Age of Ignorance, which was: when some Arab caravans entered a valley at night, they would say: "I seek refuge with the master of this valley from the evil of the fools of his people!" And they believed that by saying this, the chief of the Jinn would protect them from the evil of the foolish Jinn! [^1] And since superstitions are the source of increased intellectual degradation, fear, and error, the sentence "فَزادُوهُمْ رَهَقاً" (so they increased them in Rahaq) appears at the end of the verse.

Additionally, it is understood from this verse that the tribe of Jinn also has males and females, as it uses the expression "men from among the Jinn" (رجال من الجن). [^2] In any case, the concept of the verse is a broad one that includes any form of humans seeking refuge with Jinn, and the above superstition is one instance of it, just as we know there were many soothsayers among the Arabs who believed they could solve many problems and give news of the future through the tribe of "Jinn."

[^1]: "Majma' al-Bayan," Vol. 10, p. 369 and "Ruh al-Ma'ani," Vol. 28, p. 85. [^2]: There is another explanation in the commentary of the above verse that a group of commentators have mentioned as a possibility, which is: the seeking of refuge by a group of humans with the Jinn caused the Jinn to become rebellious, and they imagined themselves to be the source and origin of important matters. However, the first interpretation seems more correct (according to the first interpretation, the pronoun in "zadu" refers to the "Jinn" and the pronoun "hum" refers to the humans, contrary to the second interpretation).

Ayahs

وَأَنَّهُمْ ظَنُّوا۟ كَمَا ظَنَنتُمْ أَن لَّن يَبْعَثَ ٱللَّهُ أَحَدًا (٧)
They thought, just as you think, that Allah will not raise anyone from the dead. (7)
وَأَنَّا لَمَسْنَا ٱلسَّمَآءَ فَوَجَدْنَـٰهَا مُلِئَتْ حَرَسًا شَدِيدًا وَشُهُبًا (٨)
Indeed we made for the heaven and found it full of mighty sentries and flames. (8)
وَأَنَّا كُنَّا نَقْعُدُ مِنْهَا مَقَـٰعِدَ لِلسَّمْعِ ۖ فَمَن يَسْتَمِعِ ٱلْـَٔانَ يَجِدْ لَهُۥ شِهَابًا رَّصَدًا (٩)
We used to sit in its positions to eavesdrop, but anyone listening now finds a flame waiting for him. (9)
وَأَنَّا لَا نَدْرِىٓ أَشَرٌّ أُرِيدَ بِمَن فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ أَمْ أَرَادَ بِهِمْ رَبُّهُمْ رَشَدًا (١٠)
We do not know whether ill is intended for those who are on the earth, or whether their Lord intends good for them. (10)

Commentary

We Used to Eavesdrop, But...

These verses continue the discourse of the believers among the jinn, which they expressed while preaching to their people, inviting them toward Islam and the Quran through various means. First, they say: "And a group of humans, like you, thought that Allah would never send any human (after Moses and Jesus) as a prophet" (وَ أَنَّهُمْ ظَنُّوا كَما ظَنَنْتُمْ أَنْ لَنْ يَبْعَثَ اللَّهُ أَحَداً).

Consequently, they rose to deny the Quran and reject the prophethood of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله. However, when we carefully listened to the verses of this Heavenly Book, we clearly perceived its truthfulness. Beware lest you, like the polytheists among humans, choose the path of disbelief and meet the same fate as them.

This expression serves as a warning to the polytheists, so they may realize that when the jinn possess such logic and judgment, they should awaken and cling to the mantle of the Quran and the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله.

Some have suggested that the phrase "أَنْ لَنْ يَبْعَثَ اللَّهُ أَحَداً" (that Allah would never send anyone) refers to the denial of the Resurrection rather than the denial of the mission of the prophets. Furthermore, some have stated that this verse and the preceding one are entirely the words of Allah, not the believing jinn, and are placed as parenthetical statements in the middle of their speech, with the addressees being the Arab polytheists.

According to this interpretation, the meaning of the verse would be: O Arab polytheists, the jinn also imagined, as you did, that Allah would not send any messenger; but after hearing the Quran, they realized their mistake—it is time for you to awaken as well.

However, this interpretation seems very unlikely; rather, it is apparent that these words are a continuation of the speech of the believing jinn, and the addressees are the "disbelieving jinn."

Then, the believing jinn point to one of the signs of the truth of their words, which is perceptible to all jinn in the world of nature, saying: "We sought to reach the heavens, but we found them filled with powerful guards and flaming fires (meteors)" (وَ أَنَّا لَمَسْنَا السَّماءَ فَوَجَدْناها مُلِئَتْ حَرَساً شَدِيداً وَ شُهُباً) [1] [2].

"Before this, we used to sit in stations there to eavesdrop and receive news to inform our friends, but now, whoever tries to listen will find a flaming fire (meteor) lying in wait for him!" (وَ أَنَّا كُنَّا نَقْعُدُ مِنْها مَقاعِدَ لِلسَّمْعِ فَمَنْ يَسْتَمِعِ الْآنَ يَجِدْ لَهُ شِهاباً رَصَداً).

Is this new situation not proof of the fact that with the emergence of this Prophet and the revelation of His Heavenly Book, a great transformation has occurred in the world? Why were you previously able to eavesdrop, and now no one has the power to do so?! Does the meaning of this new state not imply that the era of devilry, soothsaying, and deception has ended, the dark night of ignorance has concluded, and the world-illuminating sun of revelation and prophethood has risen?!

[1]: "لمسنا" (lamasna) is derived from the root "لمس" (lams), the meaning of which is well-known, but here it is a metaphor for seeking and searching (Mufradat al-Raghib, Tafsir al-Kabir of Fakhr al-Razi, and Tafsir al-Qurtubi).

[2]: "حرس" (haras) is the plural of "حارس" (haris), meaning guard; some have considered it a collective noun.

"شهاب" (shihab) originally means a flame that leaps from a fire, and the fiery streaks that appear in the sky as long lines are also called "shihab" (meteors). According to modern scientific research, they are small pieces of rock moving in space outside the Earth's sphere. When they approach the Earth, they fall under the influence of its gravity and plummet rapidly toward it. When they enter the atmosphere—the layer of compressed air surrounding the Earth—due to intense friction, they become hot and fiery, turning into a burning flame, and eventually, their ash settles on the ground.

In the verses of the Holy Quran, this issue is repeatedly mentioned: that "meteors" are missiles cast at the devils who intend to eavesdrop in the heavens.

Regarding what is meant by "eavesdropping" and how the jinn and devils are driven from the celestial scene by meteors, we have provided detailed discussions under verse 18 of Surah Al-Hijr (Vol. 11, p. 40 onwards) and under verse 10 of Surah As-Saffat (Vol. 19, p. 15 onwards).

In any case, the word "رصد" (rasad) means readiness to wait for something, which in Persian is expressed as "lying in wait" (کمین کردن). This word sometimes appears as an active participle, meaning someone or something sitting in ambush, and it is used in this sense in the above verses.

Then they added: "With these circumstances, we do not know whether evil is intended for those on earth, or whether their Lord intends for them guidance" (وَ أَنَّا لا نَدْرِي أَ شَرٌّ أُرِيدَ بِمَنْ فِي الْأَرْضِ أَمْ أَرادَ بِهِمْ رَبُّهُمْ رَشَداً).

In other words, we do not know whether this matter is a prelude to the descent of punishment and calamity from Allah, or a prelude to their guidance.

However, the believing jinn should have logically understood that the prohibition of eavesdropping, which coincided with the emergence of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, was a prelude to the guidance of humanity and the dismantling of the apparatus of soothsaying and other such superstitions. This could be nothing other than the announcement of the end of a dark era and the beginning of a luminous one.

But since the "jinn" had a particular attachment to the matter of eavesdropping, they could not yet believe that this deprivation was a form of goodness and blessing; otherwise, it is clear that the soothsayers in the Age of Ignorance (Jahiliyyah), relying on this very eavesdropping, played a major role in misleading the people.

It is noteworthy that in this sentence, they attribute guidance to Allah, but they mention "evil" in the passive voice without attributing it to Him. This indicates that what comes from Allah is goodness and guidance, while evil and corruption arise from the people themselves and their misuse of Divine blessings and the gifts of creation.

Although the word "goodness" (khayr) should have been mentioned in contrast to "evil" (sharr), since the word "goodness" here meant "guidance" (rashad), that specific manifestation was emphasized.

Ayahs

وَأَنَّا مِنَّا ٱلصَّـٰلِحُونَ وَمِنَّا دُونَ ذَٰلِكَ ۖ كُنَّا طَرَآئِقَ قِدَدًا (١١)
Among us some are righteous and some are otherwise: we are various sects. (11)
وَأَنَّا ظَنَنَّآ أَن لَّن نُّعْجِزَ ٱللَّهَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَلَن نُّعْجِزَهُۥ هَرَبًا (١٢)
We know that we cannot frustrate Allah on the earth, nor can we frustrate Him by fleeing. (12)
وَأَنَّا لَمَّا سَمِعْنَا ٱلْهُدَىٰٓ ءَامَنَّا بِهِۦ ۖ فَمَن يُؤْمِنۢ بِرَبِّهِۦ فَلَا يَخَافُ بَخْسًا وَلَا رَهَقًا (١٣)
When we heard the [message of] guidance, we believed in it. Whoever that has faith in his Lord will fear neither privation nor oppression. (13)
وَأَنَّا مِنَّا ٱلْمُسْلِمُونَ وَمِنَّا ٱلْقَـٰسِطُونَ ۖ فَمَنْ أَسْلَمَ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ تَحَرَّوْا۟ رَشَدًا (١٤)
Among us some are Muslims and some of us are perverse.” ’ Those who submit [to Allah]—it is they who pursue rectitude. (14)
وَأَمَّا ٱلْقَـٰسِطُونَ فَكَانُوا۟ لِجَهَنَّمَ حَطَبًا (١٥)
As for the perverse, they will be firewood for hell. (15)

Commentary

We Heard the Truth and Submitted

These verses continue the discourse of the believing Jinn as they preach to their straying people. In the first verse, it speaks from their tongue: "Among us are those who are righteous and those who are otherwise; we are of various and different groups" (وَ أَنَّا مِنَّا الصَّالِحُونَ وَ مِنَّا دُونَ ذلِكَ كُنَّا طَرائِقَ قِدَداً).

They likely said this so that the presence of "Iblis" among the Jinn tribe would not lead some to the misconception that the nature of Jinn is inherently rooted in evil, corruption, and mischief, and that the light of guidance could never shine upon their hearts.

With this statement, the believing Jinn clarify that the principle of free will and choice also governs them. Both righteous and unrighteous individuals exist among them; therefore, the grounds for guidance are present within their existence. Fundamentally, one of the factors in effective preaching is "granting dignity" to the other party and drawing their attention to the existence of potential for guidance and perfection.

There is also the possibility that the believing Jinn said this to exonerate themselves from the issue of abusing the matter of eavesdropping [on the heavens]. That is: although some of us used to provide information obtained through eavesdropping to wicked humans to cause people's misguidance, not all of the Jinn tribe were like that.

Furthermore, this verse corrects our human perceptions regarding the Jinn, because in the minds of many people, the word "Jinn" is associated with a kind of "mischief," corruption, misguidance, and deviation. This verse states that they also have different groups: righteous and unrighteous.

The word "qidad" (rhyming with pisar) is the plural of "qidd" (rhyming with zidd), meaning "cut off." It is also applied to different groups because they are segments separated from one another.

The believing Jinn continue their speech by warning others, saying: "And we are certain that we can never frustrate Allah on earth, nor can we frustrate Him by flight" (وَ أَنَّا ظَنَنَّا أَنْ لَنْ نُعْجِزَ اللَّهَ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَ لَنْ نُعْجِزَهُ هَرَباً). Therefore, if you imagine that you can escape Allah's punishment and penalty by fleeing to a corner of the earth or a point in the heavens, you are gravely mistaken.

In this way, the first phrase refers to escaping from the grasp of Allah's power on earth, and the second phrase refers to absolute flight, whether on earth or in the heavens.

There is also another possibility in the interpretation of the verse: the first refers to the fact that one cannot prevail over Allah, and the second refers to the fact that one cannot flee from the grasp of His justice. Thus, when there is neither a way to prevail nor a way to escape, there is no choice but to submit to the command of His justice.

The believing Jinn add in the continuation of their words: "And when we heard the guidance of the Quran, we believed in it" (وَ أَنَّا لَمَّا سَمِعْنَا الْهُدى‌ آمَنَّا بِهِ‌). And if we are calling you to the guidance of the Quran, we have already acted upon this program ourselves; therefore, we do not invite others to something that we ourselves have abandoned.

Then, they state the result of faith in one short sentence, saying: "Whoever believes in his Lord shall fear neither loss nor oppression" (فَمَنْ يُؤْمِنْ بِرَبِّهِ فَلا يَخافُ بَخْساً وَ لا رَهَقاً).

"Bakhs" (rhyming with shakhs) means "reduction through injustice and oppression," and "rahaq" (rhyming with shafaq), as previously mentioned, means "covering something by force."

Some have stated the difference between these two expressions as follows: "bakhs" refers to the fact that nothing will be diminished from their "good deeds," and "rahaq" refers to the fact that nothing will be added to their "evil deeds."

Others have considered "bakhs" as referring to a deficiency in "good deeds" and "rahaq" as referring to "burdensome obligations."

In any case, the meaning is: whatever small or large deed the believers perform, they shall receive its reward in full without any reduction.

It is true that the justice of the Lord is not limited to believers, but since non-believers have no righteous deeds, no mention is made of their reward.

In the next verse, to further explain the fate of believers and disbelievers, they say: "Through the guidance of the Quran, we know that among us are those who are Muslim (submitted) and among us are the unjust and wrongdoers" (وَ أَنَّا مِنَّا الْمُسْلِمُونَ وَ مِنَّا الْقاسِطُونَ‌) [1]

[1]: "Qasit" is from the root "qist," meaning equitable distribution. When this root is used in the form of "If'al" (Iqsat), it means the implementation of justice, but when used in the simple triliteral form (as in the above verse), it means injustice and deviation from the path of truth.

"But those who choose Islam have sought out the right way and have stepped toward guidance and divine reward" (فَمَنْ أَسْلَمَ فَأُولئِكَ تَحَرَّوْا رَشَداً) [1]

"And as for the unjust, they are firewood for Hell" (وَ أَمَّا الْقاسِطُونَ فَكانُوا لِجَهَنَّمَ حَطَباً). It is noteworthy that in these verses, "Muslim" is placed in contrast to "unjust," indicating that what restrains a person from injustice is faith itself; otherwise, a faithless person will inevitably be tainted by injustice and oppression. Furthermore, it shows that a true believer is one who is never tainted by injustice, as we read in a hadith from the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله:

"The believer is he from whom people feel secure regarding their lives and their wealth." [2] And in another hadith from the same Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, it is stated:

"The Muslim is he from whose tongue and hand other Muslims are safe." [3] The expression "taharraw rashada" shows that believers pursue guidance with attention, intent,

[1]: "Taharraw" is from the root "taharri," meaning to intend or seek something.

[2]: Tafsir "Ruh al-Bayan," Vol. 10, p. 195.

[3]: "Usul al-Kafi," Vol. 2, Chapter: The Believer, His Signs and Attributes.

and research, not blindly or ignorantly. Their highest reward is attaining the truth, under the shadow of which they attain all divine blessings, while the worst misery of the oppressors is that they are the firewood of Hell—meaning the fire flares up from within their very being!

Ayahs

وَأَلَّوِ ٱسْتَقَـٰمُوا۟ عَلَى ٱلطَّرِيقَةِ لَأَسْقَيْنَـٰهُم مَّآءً غَدَقًا (١٦)
If they are steadfast on the path [of Allah], We shall provide them with abundant water, (16)
لِّنَفْتِنَهُمْ فِيهِ ۚ وَمَن يُعْرِضْ عَن ذِكْرِ رَبِّهِۦ يَسْلُكْهُ عَذَابًا صَعَدًا (١٧)
so that We may test them therein, and whoever turns away from the remembrance of his Lord, He will let him into an escalating punishment. (17)
وَأَنَّ ٱلْمَسَـٰجِدَ لِلَّهِ فَلَا تَدْعُوا۟ مَعَ ٱللَّهِ أَحَدًا (١٨)
The places of worship belong to Allah, so do not invoke anyone along with Allah. (18)
وَأَنَّهُۥ لَمَّا قَامَ عَبْدُ ٱللَّهِ يَدْعُوهُ كَادُوا۟ يَكُونُونَ عَلَيْهِ لِبَدًا (١٩)
When the servant of Allah rose to pray to Him, they almost crowded around him. (19)

Commentary

We Shall Test You With These Abundant Blessings

These verses are apparently a continuation of the words of the believing Jinn to their people (although a group of commentators consider them to be the words of Allah, placed as a parenthetical statement amidst the words of the Jinn). However, since being parenthetical is contrary to the apparent context, and the phrasing of the verses bears a strong resemblance to the tone of the previous verses—which were the words of the believing Jinn—it is unlikely that they are not from the speech of the Jinn [^1]. In any case, while the previous verses spoke of the rewards for the believers in the Hereafter, these verses speak of their worldly rewards, stating: "And if they (Jinn and mankind) remain steadfast on the Path, We would certainly give them to drink of abundant water" (وَ أَنْ لَوِ اسْتَقامُوا عَلَى الطَّرِيقَةِ لَأَسْقَيْناهُمْ ماءً غَدَقاً).

We would pour down the rain of Our mercy upon them and place at their disposal the sources and springs of life-giving water; and where there is abundant water, everything is abundant. In this way, We would encompass them with various types of blessings.

"Ghadaq" (ghadaqan) means abundant water.

[^1]: It seems the only thing that led this group of commentators to consider it the word of God and a parenthetical statement are the "first-person plural" pronouns used in these verses. In one place it says: "We give them abundant water to drink," and in another, it says: "The purpose is that We may test them." However, if we consider these expressions as a "narration of meaning" (naql bi-l-ma'na), no problem arises. It is exactly like someone narrating from his friend and saying: "So-and-so believes that I am a good person" (of course, the friend did not use the word "I," but rather "he," yet the narrator chooses such an expression).

This is one of several times the Holy Quran emphasizes that "faith and piety" are not only the source of "spiritual blessings" but also cause an increase in material provisions, abundance of grace, prosperity, development, and "material blessing." We had a detailed discussion on this subject in this same volume in the commentary of Surah Nuh, under verse 12, titled "The Relationship between Faith and Piety with Prosperity and Development."

It is noteworthy that according to this statement, what brings about the abundance of blessings is steadfastness in faith, not just the principle of faith itself. This is because temporary and fleeting faith cannot manifest such blessings; the important thing is steadfastness in faith and piety, where many people's feet are faltering and shaky.

In the next verse, referring to another reality in this regard, it adds: "The purpose is that We may test them therewith" (لِنَفْتِنَهُمْ فِيهِ).

Will the increase in blessings become a source of pride and negligence for them? Or will it be a cause for awakening, gratitude, and greater attention toward Allah?

From here, it becomes clear that one of the important means of Divine trial is the abundance of blessings. Incidentally, testing by means of "blessing" is harder and more complex than testing by means of "punishment." This is because the nature of increased blessings is to bring about lethargy, laziness, negligence, and drowning in pleasures and desires; and this is exactly what distances man from Allah and prepares the field for Satan's activity. Only those can remain safe from the undesirable side effects of abundant blessings who are constantly in remembrance of Allah, do not forget His mention, and protect the house of the heart from the influence of devils through continuous reminders [^1]. Therefore, following that, it adds: "And whosoever turns away from the remembrance of his Lord, He will thrust him into a severe and ever-increasing punishment" (وَ مَنْ يُعْرِضْ عَنْ ذِكْرِ رَبِّهِ يَسْلُكْهُ عَذاباً صَعَداً).

"Sa'ad" means to ascend and go up, and sometimes it refers to a "mountain pass." Since climbing mountain passes is arduous, this word is used to mean "arduous matters." Thus, many commentators have interpreted the above verse in this way, meaning "an arduous punishment," similar to what appears in verse 17 of Surah Al-Muddaththir regarding some of the polytheists:

سَأُرْهِقُهُ صَعُوداً: "I will cover him with an arduous punishment."

However, there is a possibility that the above expression, while stating the arduousness of this punishment, also refers to its being ever-increasing.

In this way, the above verses, on one hand, state the relationship between faith and piety with the increase of blessings; on the other hand, the relationship between the increase of blessings and Divine trials; and on a third hand, the relationship between turning away from the remembrance of Allah and an arduous, increasing punishment. These are realities pointed out in other verses of the Quran as well.

As we read in verse 124 of Surah Ta-Ha: وَ مَنْ أَعْرَضَ عَنْ ذِكْرِي فَإِنَّ لَهُ مَعِيشَةً ضَنْكاً: "And whoever turns away from My remembrance—indeed, he will have a narrow and difficult life."

[^1]: Some commentators have suggested the possibility that "the Path" (al-tariqah) in the above verse refers to the path of disbelief, and the increase in blessings following steadfastness in this path is actually a prelude to punishment and an instance of "gradual entrapment" (istidraj) in blessings. However, this interpretation is not at all consistent with the tone of the verse under discussion or the verses before and after it.

And in verse 40 of Surah Al-Naml, we read from the tongue of Solomon (peace be upon him): هذا مِنْ فَضْلِ رَبِّي لِيَبْلُوَنِي أَ أَشْكُرُ أَمْ أَكْفُرُ: "This is from the favor of my Lord to test me whether I will be grateful or ungrateful."

And in verse 28 of Surah Al-Anfal, it says: وَ اعْلَمُوا أَنَّما أَمْوالُكُمْ وَ أَوْلادُكُمْ فِتْنَةٌ: "And know that your properties and your children are but a trial."

In the next verse, it says from the tongue of the believing Jinn when inviting others to monotheism: "And that the mosques are for Allah, so do not invoke anyone along with Allah" (وَ أَنَّ الْمَساجِدَ لِلَّهِ فَلا تَدْعُوا مَعَ اللَّهِ أَحَداً).

Regarding what is meant by "mosques" (masajid) here, various interpretations have been mentioned:

First: It refers to the places where prostration (sujud) is performed for Allah, the most perfect instance being Masjid al-Haram, and other instances being all other mosques. Its broadest meaning includes all places where a person prays and prostrates for Allah, and according to the famous Hadith of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله who said:

"The entire earth has been made for me a place of prostration (masjid) and a means of purification (tahur)" [^1], it includes everywhere.

Thus, it is a response to the actions of the Arab polytheists and their like who had turned the Kaaba into an idol temple, and to the actions of deviant Christians who went toward "the Trinity" and worshipped three gods in their churches. The Quran says: All places of worship belong exclusively to Allah, and in these temples, prostration can only be performed for Allah, and the worship of anyone else is forbidden.

Second: It refers to the seven parts of the body used in prostration. These parts must be placed on the ground only for Allah, and it is not permissible for anyone else. As narrated in a famous Hadith from the ninth Imam, Muhammad ibn Ali al-Jawad عليه السلام, that the Abbasid Caliph Al-Mu'tasim asked in a gathering where Sunni jurists were present: "From which part should the hand of a thief be cut?" Some said from the wrist, citing the verse of Tayammum; others said from the elbow, citing the verse of Wudu. Al-Mu'tasim asked the Imam عليه السلام for an explanation. The Imam عليه السلام first requested to be excused from answering, but when he insisted, he said: "What they said is all wrong. Only the four fingers should be cut from the joints, and the palm and the thumb should remain." When Al-Mu'tasim sought the reason, the Imam cited the words of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله that prostration must be on seven parts: the forehead, the two hands, the two knees, and the feet. He then added: "If it is cut from the wrist or elbow, no hand remains for him to prostrate with, while Allah has said: وَ أَنَّ الْمَساجِدَ لِلَّهِ, meaning these seven parts belong to Allah, and what belongs to Allah should not be severed." This statement amazed Al-Mu'tasim, and he ordered the thief's hand to be cut from the joints of the four fingers according to the Imam's ruling [^1]. Other Hadiths have also been narrated with this theme [^2], but the Hadiths narrated in this regard are mostly without a chain of narration or with a weak chain.

[^1]: Wasa'il al-Shia, Vol. 2, p. 970, Hadith 3. [^1]: Wasa'il al-Shia, Vol. 18, p. 490 (Chapters on the Punishment for Theft, Chapter 4, Hadith 5). [^2]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, pp. 439 and 440.

Furthermore, there are cases of contradiction that are not easy to answer. For example, it is established among our jurists that if a "thief" steals for a second time (after the punishment has been carried out), the front part of his foot is cut, leaving the heel intact, while the big toe is also one of the seven parts of prostration. Similarly, in the case of a "Muharib" (one who wages war against God), one of the punishments is the cutting of a part of the hand and foot.

Third: It means that "mosques" refers to "prostration" (sujud) itself, meaning that prostration must always be for Allah and one cannot prostrate to anyone else.

This possibility is also contrary to the apparent meaning of the verse, and there is no evidence for it.

From the summary of what has been said, it is concluded that what agrees with the apparent meaning of the verse is the first interpretation, which is perfectly consistent with the preceding and following verses regarding monotheism and the exclusivity of worship for Allah. The second interpretation might be an expansion of the verse's concept, while for the third interpretation, no evidence exists.

Continuing this discourse to describe the extraordinary impact of the Holy Quran and the worship of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, it adds: "And that when the servant of Allah, Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله, stood up to worship Him and invoke Him, they (the Jinn) almost swarmed over him in a dense crowd" (وَ أَنَّهُ لَمَّا قامَ عَبْدُ اللَّهِ يَدْعُوهُ كادُوا يَكُونُونَ عَلَيْهِ لِبَداً) [^1]. "Libad" refers to something whose parts are piled on top of each other. This expression describes the amazing rush of the believing Jinn to hear the Quran upon their first encounter with it, and also describes the extraordinary attraction of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله prayer.

Two other interpretations have also been given for this verse: First, that the believing Jinn are describing the state of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله companions—how, despite their small number in Mecca, they would climb over each other's shoulders to hear his words; the purpose being that the Jinn tribe should take them as an example and hasten toward faith.

The other is that it describes the state of the Arab polytheists, who, when the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was busy with prayer and the Quran, would surround him closely to mock and harass him.

However, the latter interpretation does not fit well with the goal of the Jinn preachers who wanted to encourage others to faith, and one of the first two meanings is more appropriate.

Key Point: Distortion in the Interpretation of the Verse "And that the Mosques are for Allah"

The issue of "Tawassul" (seeking a means) to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the Guardians of the religion عليهم السلام—meaning making them a means and intercessors before Allah—is a matter that neither contradicts the reality of monotheism nor the verses of the Holy Quran. Rather, it is an emphasis on monotheism and the fact that everything comes from Allah. In the verses of the Quran, the issue of intercession (Shafa'ah) as well as the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله seeking forgiveness for the believers is repeatedly mentioned [^1].

[^1]: According to this interpretation, and assuming this sentence is from the words of the believing Jinn, the use of the "third-person" pronoun instead of the "first-person" is a rhetorical shift (iltifat), or because a group of them was describing the state of another group (take note). [^1]: Regarding the issue of "Intercession from the perspective of Quran and Hadith," we have discussed it in detail in Volume 1 under verse 48 of Surah Al-Baqarah, and regarding the reality of "Tawassul" in Volume 4 under verse 35 of Surah Al-Ma'idah.

Despite this, some who are far removed from the teachings of Islam and the Quran insist on denying any form of Tawassul and intercession, and have found pretexts to prove their point, including the above verse (وَ أَنَّ الْمَساجِدَ لِلَّهِ فَلا تَدْعُوا مَعَ اللَّهِ أَحَداً). They say: According to this verse, the Quran commands not to mention anyone's name alongside Allah's name, not to call upon anyone but Him, and not to seek intercession! But in fairness, this verse has no connection to their claim; rather, its goal is to negate polytheism—that is, placing something on the same level as Allah in worship or in seeking a need.

In other words: if someone truly asks for God's work from someone other than God and considers them the independent authority in performing it, they are a polytheist, just as the word "with" (ma'a) in the phrase "do not invoke with Allah" testifies to this meaning—that one should not consider anyone "on par" with Allah or as an "independent source of influence."

However, if one asks for intercession from the Prophets of God, or requests mediation before the Lord, not only does the verse not negate it, but the Quran sometimes invites the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله himself to this meaning, and sometimes commands others to seek intercession from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله.

In verse 103 of Al-Tawbah, we read: خُذْ مِنْ أَمْوالِهِمْ صَدَقَةً تُطَهِّرُهُمْ وَ تُزَكِّيهِمْ بِها وَ صَلِّ عَلَيْهِمْ إِنَّ صَلاتَكَ سَكَنٌ لَهُمْ: "Take alms from their wealth to purify them and sanctify them thereby, and pray for them (when taking the alms), for your prayer is a source of comfort for them."

And in verse 97 of Surah Yusuf, we read from the tongue of his brothers addressing their father: يا أَبانَا اسْتَغْفِرْ لَنا ذُنُوبَنا إِنَّا كُنَّا خاطِئِينَ: "O our father! Ask forgiveness for our sins for us; indeed, we have been sinners."

Jacob (peace be upon him) did not deny this request but gave them a favorable promise and said: سَوْفَ أَسْتَغْفِرُ لَكُمْ رَبِّي: "I will soon ask my Lord for forgiveness for you."

Therefore, the issue of Tawassul and seeking intercession in the sense explained is among the explicit commands of the Quran.

Ayahs

قُلْ إِنَّمَآ أَدْعُوا۟ رَبِّى وَلَآ أُشْرِكُ بِهِۦٓ أَحَدًا (٢٠)
Say, ‘I pray only to my Lord, and I do not ascribe any partner to Him.’ (20)
قُلْ إِنِّى لَآ أَمْلِكُ لَكُمْ ضَرًّا وَلَا رَشَدًا (٢١)
Say, ‘I have no power to bring you any harm or good [of my own accord].’ (21)
قُلْ إِنِّى لَن يُجِيرَنِى مِنَ ٱللَّهِ أَحَدٌ وَلَنْ أَجِدَ مِن دُونِهِۦ مُلْتَحَدًا (٢٢)
Say, ‘Neither can anyone shelter me from Allah, nor can I find any refuge besides Him. (22)
إِلَّا بَلَـٰغًا مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ وَرِسَـٰلَـٰتِهِۦ ۚ وَمَن يَعْصِ ٱللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُۥ فَإِنَّ لَهُۥ نَارَ جَهَنَّمَ خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَآ أَبَدًا (٢٣)
[I have no duty] except to transmit from Allah, and [to communicate] His messages; and whoever disobeys Allah and His apostle, there will indeed be for him the fire of hell, to remain in it forever.’ (23)
حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا رَأَوْا۟ مَا يُوعَدُونَ فَسَيَعْلَمُونَ مَنْ أَضْعَفُ نَاصِرًا وَأَقَلُّ عَدَدًا (٢٤)
When they see what they are promised, they will know who is weaker in supporters and fewer in numbers. (24)

Commentary

Say: I Possess No Power of Benefit or Harm for Anyone

In these verses, to consolidate the foundations of Monotheism (Tawhid) and to negate every form of polytheism (Shirk) alluded to in the previous verses, He first commands the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family): "Say: I only call upon my Lord and I do not associate anyone with Him" (قُلْ إِنَّما أَدْعُوا رَبِّي وَ لا أُشْرِكُ بِهِ أَحَداً).

Then He commands: "Say: I possess no power of harm or right guidance for you" (قُلْ إِنِّی لا أَمْلِكُ لَكُمْ ضَرًّا وَ لا رَشَداً).

And He further adds: "Say: Indeed, no one can protect me against Allah [if I were to disobey Him], nor can I find any refuge besides Him" (قُلْ إِنِّي لَنْ يُجِيرَنِي مِنَ اللَّهِ أَحَدٌ وَ لَنْ أَجِدَ مِنْ دُونِهِ مُلْتَحَداً) [1]. Thus, neither can anyone grant me asylum, nor can anything serve as a sanctuary. These statements, on one hand, are an admission of complete servitude before Allah; on the other hand, they negate any form of "extremism" (ghuluw) regarding the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family). Thirdly, they demonstrate that not only are idols powerless, but even the person of the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family), despite all his greatness, cannot be an independent refuge against Allah's punishment. Fourthly, it puts an end to the excuses and unreasonable expectations of stubborn individuals who demanded divine acts from the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family), proving that intercession (shafa'ah) and seeking a means (tawassul) are also only by Allah's permission.

"Multahad" (ملتحد) means a secure refuge. Originally, it is derived from the root "lahd" (لحد) (rhyming with mahd), meaning a cavity situated to one side, similar to what is dug for the deceased at the bottom of a grave—where they advance slightly into one side of the grave's depth to place the body so that soil is not poured directly onto it and it remains better protected from animals. Subsequently, it was applied to any other secure place or refuge.

As we noted in the previous verses, the purpose of these expressions is that the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) has no independent role against Allah. At the same time, he can ask Allah to solve people's problems or request guidance for worthy individuals, and this is the essence of Monotheism, not polytheism.

It is noteworthy that in these verses, "guidance" (rushd) is placed in contrast to "harm" (darr), indicating that true benefit and profit lie in guidance, just as in the speech of the Jinn in the preceding verses, "guidance" (rushd) was contrasted with "evil" (sharr); both are consistent with one another.

In the following verse, He adds: "My duty is only to convey [the truth] from Allah and His messages" (إِلَّا بَلاغاً مِنَ اللَّهِ وَ رِسالاتِهِ) [1]. This expression is similar to what is frequently mentioned in the verses of the Quran.

[^1]: Some have mentioned a cause of revelation (shan nuzul) for this verse: that the pagans of Quraish told the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family), "Return from your religion so that we may give you protection." This verse was revealed as a response to them (Tafsir Abu al-Futuh al-Razi, Vol. 11, p. 293).

[^1]: Since "balagh" (conveying) usually takes the preposition "an" (from), some have interpreted "min" here to mean "an," while others consider the word "ka'inan" (originating) to be implied (except for a conveyance originating from Allah).

As we read in Surah Al-Ma'idah, verse 92: "The duty of Our Messenger is only the clear conveyance" (أَنَّما عَلى‌ رَسُولِنَا الْبَلاغُ الْمُبِينُ). And in Surah Al-A'raf, verse 188: "Say: I possess no power of benefit or harm for myself, except what Allah wills. Had I known the unseen, I would have acquired much wealth, and no evil would have touched me. I am but a warner and a bringer of glad tidings to a people who believe" (قُلْ لا أَمْلِكُ لِنَفْسِي نَفْعاً وَ لا ضَرًّا إِلَّا ما شاءَ اللَّهُ وَ لَوْ كُنْتُ أَعْلَمُ الْغَيْبَ لَاسْتَكْثَرْتُ مِنَ الْخَيْرِ وَ ما مَسَّنِيَ السُّوءُ إِنْ أَنَا إِلَّا نَذِيرٌ وَ بَشِيرٌ لِقَوْمٍ يُؤْمِنُونَ).

Another interpretation has been mentioned by a group of commentators for this sentence: "I have no refuge for my own salvation except in conveying the call of Truth and fulfilling His mission" [1]. Regarding the difference between "balagh" (conveyance) and "risalat" (messages), some have said "balagh" refers to conveying the principles of religion (usul), while "risalat" refers to explaining the branches of religion (furu').

Others have considered "balagh" to mean conveying divine commands and "risalat" to mean their implementation.

However, it seems that both return to the same meaning and reinforce one another, evidenced by several verses of the Quran that mention both as a single concept, such as verse 62 of Al-A'raf: "I convey (uballighukum) to you the messages (risalat) of my Lord" (أُبَلِّغُكُمْ رِسالاتِ رَبِّي).

In any case, at the end of the verse, He warns: "And whoever disobeys Allah and His Messenger, then indeed for him is the fire of Hell, to abide therein forever" (وَ مَنْ يَعْصِ اللَّهَ وَ رَسُولَهُ فَإِنَّ لَهُ نارَ جَهَنَّمَ خالِدِينَ فِيها أَبَداً).

It is clear that this does not refer to every sinner, but rather to the polytheists and disbelievers, as not every sinner deserves eternal residence in the fire of Hell.

Then He adds: "This state of the disbelievers and polytheists—who constantly mock the Muslims and consider them weak—will continue until they see what they have been promised; then they will know whose helper is weaker and whose numbers are fewer" (حَتَّى إِذا رَأَوْا ما يُوعَدُونَ فَسَيَعْلَمُونَ مَنْ أَضْعَفُ ناصِراً وَ أَقَلُّ عَدَداً) [1]. Regarding whether "what they are promised" refers to punishment in this world, the hereafter, or both, various interpretations have been given. However, it is appropriate for the meaning to be general and broad, especially since the abundance or scarcity of numbers and the strength or weakness of a helper mostly pertain to this world. Thus, a group of commentators has interpreted it as the Battle of Badr, where the strength and power of the Muslims became apparent, and in several narrations, it has been interpreted as the reappearance of the Mahdi (may our souls be sacrificed for him). Therefore, if we interpret the verse in its broad sense, it encompasses all of these.

Furthermore, in Surah Maryam, verse 75, it is stated:

[^1]: According to this interpretation, this sentence is an exception to the previous phrase "nor can I find any refuge besides Him," while according to the first interpretation, it is an exception to the previous verse.

[^1]: "Hatta" (until) is usually used to express the end or goal of something. Here, two possibilities are mentioned: First, that the goal refers to a deleted sentence, implied as: "And they will continue to mock and weaken the believers until they see what they are promised..." Second, that it is the goal for "they were gathered against him in crowds" mentioned a few verses prior. The first possibility is more appropriate.

"Until, when they see what they are promised—either the punishment [in this world] or the Hour [of Resurrection]—they will know who is worse in position and weaker in forces" (حَتَّى إِذا رَأَوْا ما يُوعَدُونَ إِمَّا الْعَذابَ وَ إِمَّا السَّاعَةَ فَسَيَعْلَمُونَ مَنْ هُوَ شَرٌّ مَكاناً وَ أَضْعَفُ جُنْداً).

In any case, the tone of the verse clearly shows that the enemies of Islam were constantly boasting of their power and large numbers, regarding the Muslims as weak and helpless. Through this, the Quran comforts and gives tidings to the believers that eventually the day of their victory and the defeat and helplessness of the enemies will arrive.

Key Points

1- The Sincerity of Divine Leaders

One of the characteristics of "Divine Leaders" is that, unlike "Satanic leaders," they are never boastful, self-important, arrogant, or conceited.

While Pharaoh raised the foolish cry of "I am your most exalted lord!" (أَنَا رَبُّكُمُ الْأَعْلى‌) and "These rivers flow beneath me" (وَ هذِهِ الْأَنْهارُ تَجْرِي مِنْ تَحْتِي), Divine Leaders, in the utmost humility, introduced themselves as small servants among the servants of Allah, possessing no power of their own against His will.

In Surah Al-Kahf, verse 110, we read: "Say: I am only a human being like you, [except that] it is revealed to me" (قُلْ إِنَّما أَنَا بَشَرٌ مِثْلُكُمْ يُوحى‌ إِلَيَ‌).

And elsewhere it says: "I do not know what will be done with me or with you. I only follow what is revealed to me, and I am but a clear warner" (احقاف 9).

In another verse, we read: "Say: I do not tell you that the treasuries of Allah are with me, nor do I know the unseen (except what Allah teaches me), nor do I claim to be an angel" (انعام 50).

Even if they reached the pinnacle of material power, they never lost their composure; like Solomon, they would say: "This is from the grace of my Lord" (هذا مِنْ فَضْلِ رَبِّي) (Naml 40).

Interestingly, in several verses of the Holy Quran, sharp expressions are seen where the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) himself is addressed, rebuking him and warning him to be strictly mindful of his responsibilities.

The collection of these verses and the preceding ones—which are not few in the Quran—is a living testament to the truthfulness of this great Prophet. Otherwise, what would have prevented him, in front of a mass of people ready to accept any status for him, from claiming grand positions beyond human thought and beyond any questioning, as history has shown many examples of regarding Satanic leaders?

Yes, expressions such as the verses under discussion are living evidence of the truthfulness of the Messenger of Allah's call.

2- Large Numbers Are Not Important; The Faith of the People Is

In the verses of the Quran, it is frequently observed that the tyrants (Taghuts) of every age boasted of their numbers and large populations to others, pridefully displaying them against the Prophets.

Pharaoh, in belittling the followers of Moses, said: "Indeed, these are but a small band" (إِنَّ هؤُلاءِ لَشِرْذِمَةٌ قَلِيلُونَ) (Ash-Shu'ara 54).

The Arab polytheists said: "We are greater in wealth and children, and we will not be punished" (نَحْنُ أَكْثَرُ أَمْوالًا وَ أَوْلاداً وَ ما نَحْنُ بِمُعَذَّبِينَ) (Saba 35).

Sometimes a faithless individual would boast of his wealth and manpower to a believer, saying: "I am greater than you in wealth and stronger in numbers" (أَنَا أَكْثَرُ مِنْكَ مالًا وَ أَعَزُّ نَفَراً) (Al-Kahf 34). But in contrast, the believers following the Prophets and Divine Leaders never relied on large numbers or manpower. Their logic was: "How many a small group has overcome a large group by Allah's permission" (كَمْ مِنْ فِئَةٍ قَلِيلةٍ غَلَبَتْ فِئَةً كَثِيرَةً بِإِذْنِ اللَّهِ) (Al-Baqarah 249).

The Commander of the Faithful, Ali (peace be upon him), says: "O people, do not be despondent on the path of guidance due to the small number of its followers" [1]. The history of the Prophets, especially the life of the Prophet of Islam (peace be upon him and his family), also shows how massive, faithless populations with all kinds of power were defeated and left helpless before the few companions of the believers. In the Holy Quran, in the stories of the "Children of Israel" and "Pharaoh," "Talut" and "Jalut," and the verses related to the battles of "Badr" and "Ahzab," this meaning is well reflected.

[^1]: Nahj al-Balagha, Sermon 201.

Ayahs

قُلْ إِنْ أَدْرِىٓ أَقَرِيبٌ مَّا تُوعَدُونَ أَمْ يَجْعَلُ لَهُۥ رَبِّىٓ أَمَدًا (٢٥)
Say, ‘I do not know if what you are promised is near, or if my Lord has set a [long] term for it.’ (25)
عَـٰلِمُ ٱلْغَيْبِ فَلَا يُظْهِرُ عَلَىٰ غَيْبِهِۦٓ أَحَدًا (٢٦)
Knower of the Unseen, He does not disclose His [knowledge of the] Unseen to anyone, (26)
إِلَّا مَنِ ٱرْتَضَىٰ مِن رَّسُولٍ فَإِنَّهُۥ يَسْلُكُ مِنۢ بَيْنِ يَدَيْهِ وَمِنْ خَلْفِهِۦ رَصَدًا (٢٧)
except to an apostle He approves of. Then He dispatches a sentinel before and behind him (27)
لِّيَعْلَمَ أَن قَدْ أَبْلَغُوا۟ رِسَـٰلَـٰتِ رَبِّهِمْ وَأَحَاطَ بِمَا لَدَيْهِمْ وَأَحْصَىٰ كُلَّ شَىْءٍ عَدَدًۢا (٢٨)
so that He may ascertain that they have communicated the messages of their Lord, and He encompasses all that is with them, and He keeps an count of all things. (28)

Commentary

Allah is the Knower of the Unseen

Since the preceding verses alluded to the fact that "the mockery and rebellion of this group shall continue until the Divine promise regarding the descent of punishment arrives," it raises the question: when will this promise be fulfilled? As commentators have noted regarding the occasion of revelation, some polytheists, such as "Nadr bin al-Harith," posed this very question after the revelation of the previous verses. The Holy Quran proceeds to answer this question, saying:

"Say: I do not know whether that which you are promised (of worldly punishment and the Resurrection) is near, or whether my Lord will appoint for it a distant term" (قُلْ إِنْ أَدْرِي أَ قَرِيبٌ ما تُوعَدُونَ أَمْ يَجْعَلُ لَهُ رَبِّي أَمَداً).

This knowledge belongs exclusively to the Pure Essence of Allah, and He has willed it to remain hidden from His servants so that the trial and testing of the creation may be completed; for if they knew whether it was near or far, the trial would lose its impact in either case.

"Amad" (rhyming with Samad) means time, with the distinction that, according to Raghib in Al-Mufradat, "Zaman" (time) encompasses both the beginning and the end, whereas "Amad" refers only to the end-point of a thing's duration.

It has also been said that "Amad" and "Abad" are close in meaning, with the difference that "Abad" encompasses an unlimited duration, while "Amad" refers to a limited period, however long it may be.

In any case, we frequently encounter this theme in the verses of the Holy Quran: whenever the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was asked about the timing of the Resurrection, he would express his lack of knowledge and say: its knowledge belongs exclusively to Allah.

It is narrated in a Hadith that one day Jibril عليه السلام appeared before the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله in the form of a desert Arab. Among the questions he asked was: "Inform me about the Hour: tell me when the Resurrection will take place?" The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله replied: "The one being questioned is no more knowledgeable (regarding this matter) than the questioner!" Once more, the man shouted loudly: "O Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله)! When is the Hour?"

The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: "Woe to you! It is surely coming; tell me, what have you prepared for it?" The Bedouin replied: "I have not prepared much prayer or fasting, but I love Allah and His Messenger."

The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: "Then you shall be with the one you love!" Anas, one of the companions of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, said: "The Muslims were never as happy about anything as they were about this Hadith." [1]

Then, continuing this discussion, He states a general rule regarding the knowledge of the Unseen (Ghayb) and says: "Allah is the Knower of the Unseen; He does not disclose His Unseen secrets to anyone."

[1]: Tafsir al-Maraghi, Vol. 29, p. 105.

(عالِمُ الْغَيْبِ فَلا يُظْهِرُ عَلى‌ غَيْبِهِ أَحَداً) [1]

Then, as an exception to this general rule, He adds: "Except to a messenger whom He has chosen and with whom He is pleased" (إِلَّا مَنِ ارْتَضى‌ مِنْ رَسُولٍ‌). To such a person, He teaches whatever He wills of the knowledge of the Unseen and conveys it to him through revelation.

"Then He sends observers and guards before him and behind him" (فَإِنَّهُ يَسْلُكُ مِنْ بَيْنِ يَدَيْهِ وَ مِنْ خَلْفِهِ رَصَداً). "Rasad" originally has a verbal noun meaning and refers to the readiness to guard something. It is also applied to the "active participle" (guard) and "passive participle" (guarded), and is used for both singular and plural—meaning it can refer to a single guard or a group of guards.

What is meant here are the angels whom Allah commands to surround His Prophet from every side after the descent of revelation, protecting and guarding him from the evil of the devils among jinn and men, their whisperings, and anything that might compromise the authenticity of the revelation, so that the Divine message reaches the servants without addition or omission and without the slightest flaw.

This is itself one of the proofs for the infallibility (Ismah) of the Prophets: that they are protected and preserved from slips and errors through unseen forces, Divine assistance, and the vigilance of His angels.

[1]: "Knower of the Unseen" (Alim al-Ghayb) is the predicate for an omitted subject, implied as "He is the Knower of the Unseen." Some have considered it an adjective or a substitute (badal) for "my Lord" in the previous verse.

In the final verse of this discussion, which is the last verse of the Surah, He explains the reason for the existence of these guards and observers as follows: "The purpose is that He may know that His messengers have conveyed the messages of their Lord [without lack or excess], and Allah encompasses all that is with them, and He has recorded the number of all things" (لِيَعْلَمَ أَنْ قَدْ أَبْلَغُوا رِسالاتِ رَبِّهِمْ وَ أَحاطَ بِما لَدَيْهِمْ وَ أَحْصى‌ كُلَّ شَيْ‌ءٍ عَدَداً) [1]. The "knowledge" mentioned here refers to "actualized knowledge." In other words, the meaning of the verse is not that Allah did not know something about His Prophets and then came to know it—for Allah's knowledge is eternal, everlasting, and infinite—rather, the intent is that this Divine knowledge should manifest externally and take an objective form; meaning that the Prophets actually convey His message and complete the proof (itmam al-hujjah).

Key Points

1- An Extensive Research into the Knowledge of the Unseen

By carefully examining various verses of the Quran, it becomes clear that there are two categories of verses regarding the knowledge of the Unseen. First are the verses that introduce the knowledge of the Unseen as exclusive to Allah and negate it from anyone else, such as verse 59 of Surah Al-An'am:

[1]: A group of commentators have returned the pronoun in "that He may know" (li-ya'lama) to the Prophet of Islam, saying the meaning is that Allah appoints guardians for the secrets of revelation so that the Prophet may know they have accurately conveyed the Divine revelation to him, leaving no doubt about its authenticity. However, considering that the "mission" (risalah) is the work of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and not the angels, and that the term "Messenger" in the previous verse and "messages" in the past few verses refer to the person of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), this interpretation seems unlikely. The first interpretation is the correct one.

وَ عِنْدَهُ مَفاتِحُ الْغَيْبِ لا يَعْلَمُها إِلَّا هُوَ: "With Him are the keys of the Unseen; none knows them but He." And verse 65 of Surah Al-Naml: قُلْ لا يَعْلَمُ مَنْ فِي السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ الْغَيْبَ إِلَّا اللَّهُ: "Say: None in the heavens and the earth knows the Unseen except Allah."

And like what is mentioned regarding the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله in verse 50 of Surah Al-An'am: قُلْ لا أَقُولُ لَكُمْ عِنْدِي خَزائِنُ اللَّهِ وَ لا أَعْلَمُ الْغَيْبَ: "Say: I do not tell you that with me are the treasures of Allah, nor do I know the Unseen." And in verse 188 of Surah Al-A'raf we read: وَ لَوْ كُنْتُ أَعْلَمُ الْغَيْبَ لَاسْتَكْثَرْتُ مِنَ الْخَيْرِ: "If I had knowledge of the Unseen, I should have multiplied all good." Finally, in verse 20 of Surah Yunus we read: فَقُلْ إِنَّمَا الْغَيْبُ لِلَّهِ: "Say: The Unseen belongs only to Allah," and similar verses.

The second category consists of verses that clearly show that the friends of Allah (Awliya) were "briefly" or "partially" aware of the Unseen. As we read in verse 179 of Surah Al-Imran: وَ ما كانَ اللَّهُ لِيُطْلِعَكُمْ عَلَى الْغَيْبِ وَ لكِنَّ اللَّهَ يَجْتَبِي مِنْ رُسُلِهِ مَنْ يَشاءُ: "Allah would not disclose to you the Unseen, but Allah chooses of His messengers whom He wills" (and places a portion of the secrets of the Unseen at his disposal).

Regarding the miracles of Prophet Jesus عليه السلام, we read that he said: وَ أُنَبِّئُكُمْ بِما تَأْكُلُونَ وَ ما تَدْرِي نَفْسٌ ما ذا تَكْسِبُ غَداً: "I inform you of what you eat and what you store in your houses" (Al-Imran: 49). The verse under discussion also shows, through the exception it contains, that Allah places a portion of the knowledge of the Unseen at the disposal of His chosen messengers (since an exception from a negation always implies an affirmation).

On the other hand, the verses of the Quran containing news of the Unseen are not few, such as verses 2 to 4 of Surah Al-Rum: غُلِبَتِ الرُّومُ فِي أَدْنَى الْأَرْضِ وَ هُمْ مِنْ بَعْدِ غَلَبِهِمْ سَيَغْلِبُونَ فِي بِضْعِ سِنِينَ: "The Romans have been defeated in the nearer land, and they, after their defeat, will be victorious within a few years." And verse 85 of Surah Al-Qasas which says: إِنَّ الَّذِي فَرَضَ عَلَيْكَ الْقُرْآنَ لَرادُّكَ إِلى‌ مَعادٍ: "He who has ordained the Quran for you will surely bring you back to the place of return (Mecca)." And verse 27 of Surah Al-Fath which says: لَتَدْخُلُنَّ الْمَسْجِدَ الْحَرامَ إِنْ شاءَ اللَّهُ آمِنِينَ: "You shall certainly enter the Sacred Mosque, if Allah wills, in security."

Fundamentally, the heavenly revelation descended upon the Prophets is a type of Unseen knowledge placed at their disposal. How can it be said they have no knowledge of the Unseen while revelation descends upon them?

Beyond all this, we have numerous narrations showing that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the Infallible Imams عليهم السلام had a general knowledge of the Unseen and would sometimes report from it. For example, in the story of the Conquest of Mecca and the incident of "Hatib bin Abi Balta'ah," who wrote a letter to the people of Mecca and gave it to a woman named "Sarah" to deliver to the polytheists to warn them of the imminent attack of the army of Islam. The woman hid the letter in her hair and set off for Mecca. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله sent Ali عليه السلام and some other Muslims after her, saying: "At a place called Rawdah Khakh, you will encounter such a woman who has a letter from Hatib to the polytheists; take the letter from her." They went and found her; at first, she vehemently denied it, but finally confessed, and they took the letter from her. [1] Similarly, the reporting of the events of the Battle of Mu'tah and the martyrdom of Ja'far and other commanders of Islam at the very moment they occurred, while the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله informed the Muslims in Medina. [2] Such instances in the life of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله are not few.

In Nahj al-Balagha, many predictions of future events are observed which show that Ali عليه السلام knew these secrets of the Unseen, such as what appears in Sermon 13 in censure of the people of Basra, where he says:

"It is as if I see your mosque like the chest of a ship, while Allah has sent punishment upon it from above and from below, and all within it are drowned."

In other narrations recorded in the books of both Sunni and Shi'a scholars, multiple predictions by him عليه السلام regarding future events are mentioned, such as what he said to "Hujr bin Qays" that he would be forced to curse him after his death [3], and what he said regarding "Marwan" that he would carry the banner of misguidance in his old age [4], and what "Kumayl bin Ziyad" said to "Hajjaj" that "the Commander of the Faithful Ali عليه السلام informed me that you are my killer" [5], and what he said regarding the Kharijites of Nahrawan that in the battle against them, not ten people from our side would be killed, and not ten from theirs would survive—and it happened exactly so. [1] Also, what he said to "Asbagh bin Nabatah" regarding the location of Imam Hussain's عليه السلام grave while passing by the land of Karbala. [2] In the book Fada'il al-Khamsah, numerous narrations from Sunni books are cited regarding the extraordinary breadth of Ali's عليه السلام knowledge, the mention of all of which would be lengthy. [3] In the narrations of the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام, multiple hadiths refer to the knowledge of the Unseen for the Infallible Imams; for instance, in the book Al-Kafi, Volume 1, in various chapters, there are explicit statements or allusions in this regard.

The late Allamah Majlisi in Bihar al-Anwar, Volume 26, has brought many hadiths, reaching up to 22 hadiths, in this field.

Altogether, the narrations regarding the awareness of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the Infallible Imams عليهم السلام of the secrets of the Unseen reach the level of tawatur (successive transmission).

Now the question is: how do we reconcile these verses and narrations, some of which negate the knowledge of the Unseen from anyone other than Allah, while others affirm it?

There are various ways to reconcile them:

1- One of the most famous ways of reconciliation is that the exclusivity of the knowledge of the Unseen to Allah refers to "essential" (dhati) and "independent" (istiqlali) knowledge. Therefore, no one else independently possesses any knowledge of the Unseen, and whatever they have is from Allah, by His grace and favor, and is "derivative" (taba'i) in nature.

The witness for this reconciliation is the very verse under discussion, which says: "Allah does not disclose the secrets of the Unseen to anyone, except the messengers with whom He is pleased." In Nahj al-Balagha, this same meaning is alluded to; when Ali عليه السلام was reporting future events (predicting the Mongol invasion of Islamic lands), one of his companions asked: "O Commander of the Faithful, do you possess knowledge of the Unseen?" The Imam smiled and said:

"This is not knowledge of the Unseen; it is learning from one who possesses knowledge (the Prophet)!" [1] This reconciliation has been accepted by many scholars and researchers.

2- The secrets of the Unseen are of two types: one part is exclusive to Allah and no one knows it but He, such as the timing of the Resurrection and similar matters; and another part He teaches to the Prophets and the Friends of Allah (Awliya). As he says in Nahj al-Balagha in the continuation of the same sermon mentioned above:

"Knowledge of the Unseen is only the knowledge of the Hour, and what Allah has enumerated in His saying: 'Verily, Allah! With Him is the knowledge of the Hour, and He sends down the rain, and He knows that which is in the wombs, and no soul knows what it will earn tomorrow, and no soul knows in what land it will die...'"

Then the Imam عليه السلام added in explanation: "Allah the Glorified knows what is in the wombs: is it male or female? Ugly or beautiful? Generous or miserly? Blessed or wretched? Is it for Hell or Paradise? ... These are the Unseen knowledges that no one knows but Allah, and other than that is the knowledge which Allah taught His Prophets and he taught to me." [1] It is possible for some humans to gain general knowledge of the state of a fetus or the falling of rain and such, but detailed knowledge and awareness of the particulars of these matters is exclusive to the Pure Essence of Allah. Just as we have general knowledge of the Resurrection, but are unaware of its details and specifics. If it is mentioned in narrations that the Prophet or the Imams reported on certain newborns or the end of certain individuals' lives, it pertains to that very general knowledge.

3- Another way to reconcile these two groups of verses and narrations is that the secrets of the Unseen are recorded in two places: in the "Preserved Tablet" (Lawh Mahfuz)—the exclusive treasury of Allah's knowledge—where no change occurs and no one is aware of it; and the "Tablet of Erasure and Affirmation" (Lawh Mahw wa Ithbat), which is knowledge of contingencies rather than the "complete cause," and for this reason, it is subject to change. What others do not know pertains to the former part.

Thus, we read in a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام:

"Verily, Allah has a knowledge that none knows but He, and a knowledge He has made known to His angels and His messengers. Whatever He has made known to His angels, His Prophets, and His messengers, we know it." [2] It is also narrated from Imam Ali bin al-Hussain عليه السلام that he said:

"If it were not for one verse in the Book of Allah, I would have told you of what was and what will be until the Day of Resurrection!" I asked him: "Which verse?" He said: "The word of Allah: 'Allah erases what He wills and affirms, and with Him is the Mother of the Book (the Preserved Tablet).'" [1] The categorization of knowledge according to this reconciliation is based on its "certainty" or "uncertainty," whereas in the previous reconciliation, it was based on the "amount" of information (take note).

4- Another way is that Allah is "actually" (bi'l-fi'l) aware of all secrets of the Unseen, but the Prophets and Awliya may not actually know many secrets of the Unseen, but when they "will" to know, Allah teaches them. Of course, this will also occurs with the permission and pleasure of Allah.

According to this reconciliation, the verses and narrations saying they do not know refer to "actual" lack of knowledge, and those saying they know refer to the "possibility" of knowing.

This is exactly like someone giving a letter to a person to deliver to another. Here, it can be said: he has no knowledge of the contents of the letter, yet he can open the letter and become informed. Sometimes the owner of the letter has given him permission to read it; in this case, he can be considered knowledgeable of the contents from one perspective, and sometimes he has not been given permission.

The witness for this reconciliation are the narrations in the book Al-Kafi in a chapter titled "Verily, when the Imams will to know, they know." Among them, we read in a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام:

"When the Imam wills to know something, Allah makes him know it." [1] This mode of reconciliation solves many problems regarding the knowledge of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the Imam عليه السلام, including: how did they eat food or drink water that was, for example, poisoned, while it is not permissible for a person to take an action that causes danger to himself? It must be said that in such cases, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله or the Imam عليه السلام did not have permission to "will" that the secrets of the Unseen be revealed to them.

Also, sometimes wisdom dictates that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله or the Imam عليه السلام should not know a matter, or a trial should take place for him that leads to his perfection. Just as it is mentioned in the story of "Laylat al-Mabit" (the night of the migration) that Ali عليه السلام slept in the Prophet's bed, while it is narrated from the Imam himself that he did not know whether he would be martyred in the morning when the polytheists of Quraysh attacked that bed or if he would survive.

Here, the wisdom is that the Imam عليه السلام should not be aware of the outcome of this act so that the Divine trial may be realized.
